_note: Estimated benchmarks — run `go test -bench=. ./pkg/passwords/` for real numbers on your hardware. Argon2id benchmarks depend heavily on memory bandwidth and parallelism.

results.note: The 100ms target is intentional — bcrypt was designed to be slow. cost=10 is the 1999-era tuning updated for modern hardware. Too fast = GPU-crackable.
2^4 = 16x slower than cost=10. Cost=14 is appropriate for high-security contexts (admin accounts, banking) where a 1.6s login delay is acceptable.
OWASP-recommended parameters for 2023. The memory requirement is the key defense: each hash needs 64MB of RAM, limiting GPU parallelism.
RTX 4090 estimated. bcrypt is compute-hard but not memory-hard — GPUs can parallelize across many cores. 10,000/sec is ~1 million x slower than SHA-256.
24GB VRAM / 64MB per hash = 375 parallel hashes maximum on RTX 4090. Memory bandwidth (not compute) is the bottleneck — this is memory-hardness in action.
10 billion/sec on RTX 4090. A 6-char lowercase password (26^6 = 308M combinations) cracks in 0.03 seconds. This is why SHA-256 must never be used for passwords.

insight: The threat model is a stolen database. SHA-256 at 10B/sec: a 6-char lowercase password cracks in 0.03 seconds. Argon2id at 375 parallel hashes on GPU: the same password takes 308M / 375 = ~820,000 seconds = 9.5 days. Peppering makes this moot — without the pepper, even 9.5 days of cracking yields nothing.
